narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 34, middle family income bracket, full-time enrollment plans, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 57.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school that year, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 34, which, while solid, pulled in the opposite direction by suggesting a stronger chance of passing compared to lower scorers. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 1, also in the bottom 10% and well under the average, further reinforced the model's concern about early academic struggles. These three elements—your later-year grades, LSAT, and early-year performance—dominated the decision, outweighing other aspects like your GPA being close to the 3.21 average or your common full-time status. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and score around 36.7 on the LSAT, your lower deciles stood out as red flags signaling potential bar exam risks. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with drive and a middle-income background; it's the model highlighting patterns from past data where similar profiles faced higher failure odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative in your personal statement addressing these early hurdles. Many students in your position have succeeded by targeting these areas, turning rejections into future acceptances. Your path to law school is still very much open with focused improvements.
